title: Pyrography nature panel
subtitle: A burned botanical subject on a sycamore panel
excerpt: A pyrography panel depicting a stylised fern frond on pale sycamore: the frond outline burned with a fine writing tip, the pinnae shaded with a curved shading tip to produce graduated tones. A project that teaches the two core pyrography skills; line control and tonal shading; on a forgiving botanical subject.
sourceNotes: Traditional European pyrography on pale hardwood panels; Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for pokerwork botanical subject conventions.

content.text: A nature panel uses fine line work and graded shading to draw a botanical subject on pale hardwood. A fern frond is an ideal first subject. It is built from the same shape (the pinnule) repeated at smaller sizes from the base to the tip. Pale sycamore is the best surface for this. The grain reads as soft texture under the fronds. The pale background carries the full tonal range, from light shading to dark line.
The panel

A sycamore panel, 20 by 15 cm and 6 mm thick. Alternatively, pale birch plywood (12 mm, sanded to 240 grit) produces a similar base. The surface must be sanded smooth and free of tool marks before burning; any scratch in the surface will show as a light line running through the burned area. Lime (basswood) is the softest and easiest option if this is a first pyrography piece.
Tools
A nichrome wire burner with a
writing tip
for outlines and a
shading tip
for the tonal work. A nichrome wire burner allows precise temperature control; important for the lighter tone areas where a solid-tip burner can easily overshoot. A cooling stand for tip changes.
Method

Transfer the design: draw the fern frond lightly in pencil on the panel. The central rachis (mid-rib) of the frond runs from bottom-centre to top-right. Each pinnule is a small oval, 1.5 cm long at the base and diminishing to 0.5 cm at the tip. Mark the mid-rib of each pinnule in pencil.

Burn the outline with the writing tip at medium temperature; the line should be a warm brown, not dark brown or black. Pull the tip along the pencil line at a consistent speed; stopping produces a dark spot. Work the rachis line first, then each pinnule outline from base to tip.

Switch to the shading tip. Shade the pinnules: starting at the darkest point (the underside of each pinnule, adjacent to the rachis) and moving toward the lighter tip. Press the shading tip flat for the darkest areas; lift it to a low angle as you move toward the lighter end. Keep the tip moving; a stationary shading tip will burn through the surface.

Add the venation: switch back to the writing tip at a lower temperature and burn the vein lines within each pinnule. Fine parallel lines running from the mid-rib to the margin, 2 mm apart. The vein lines should be lighter than the outline; reduce the temperature by 20 percent from the outline setting.

Review the tonal balance: hold the panel at arm's length. The darkest marks should be in the lower central area (base of pinnules, darker shadow side) and the lightest area at the frond tip. Add final dark accents with the writing tip at high temperature if needed.
Finishing

Leave the panel unfinished for the first 2 days after burning. If Danish oil is applied, do it after the Danish oil has set for 24 hours; oil slightly deepens the tone of the unburned wood and gives the background a warm amber that works well behind the burned design.
Variations
Oak leaf: same construction as fern, with the lobed margin and 5 or 7 lobes. Slightly more complex outline but the same shading approach.
Bird silhouette: a simple bird (wren or swift) in profile, with feather texture added using the shading tip. No internal venation lines; all texture is shading.
Care
Do not sand the finished panel. Dust with a dry cloth. Hang away from direct sunlight; prolonged UV exposure yellows unfinished sycamore and reduces the contrast with the burned marks.

attrs.intro: Common problems with the pyrography nature panel.

items.fix: Work in a single fluid stroke; do not pause mid-line. For curves, rotate the panel so each arc is a natural pulling motion.
items.cause: Hesitation during the burn stroke, or speed inconsistent through curves
items.symptom: Lines uneven; dark spots where the tip stopped or changed direction

items.fix: Reduce the temperature by 20 to 30 percent for shading work. Speed is the primary tone control; move faster for lighter areas, slower for darker.
items.cause: Temperature too high for the shading pass, or tip moving too slowly
items.symptom: Shading too dark too quickly; no tonal range

items.fix: Use a 2H pencil only and draw lightly. For panels already burned, very light sanding at 320 grit will remove pencil marks from the unburned areas without affecting the burned marks significantly.
items.cause: Pencil lines too heavy; HB or softer pencil used instead of 2H
items.symptom: Pencil lines show through the burn marks after completion

attrs.heading: What can go wrong
